From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Religious observance lasting three days

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **relies largely or entirely on a [single source](/wiki/Wikipedia:Articles_with_a_single_source "Wikipedia:Articles with a single source")**. Relevant discussion may be found on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Triduum "Talk:Triduum"). Please help [improve this article](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Triduum&action=edit) by [introducing citations to additional sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). *Find sources:* ["Triduum"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Triduum%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Triduum%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Triduum%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Triduum%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Triduum%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Triduum%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(September 2017)* |

| [Liturgical seasons](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") |
| --- |
| * Pre-Christmas   + [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent") (*Western*)   + [Nativity Fast](/wiki/Nativity_Fast "Nativity Fast") (*Byzantine*)   + [Annunciation](/wiki/Season_of_Annunciation "Season of Annunciation") (*Syriac*) * [Christmas](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide") * [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_season "Epiphany season")   + [Ordinary Time](/wiki/Ordinary_Time "Ordinary Time") (*Western*) * [Pre-Lent](/wiki/Pre-Lent "Pre-Lent") * [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") (*Western*) / [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent") (*Eastern*) * [Paschal Triduum](/wiki/Paschal_Triduum "Paschal Triduum") (*Western*) * [Easter](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide") * [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost_season "Pentecost season")   + [Ordinary Time](/wiki/Ordinary_Time "Ordinary Time") (*Western*) * [Summer](/wiki/Season_of_Summer "Season of Summer") (*East Syriac*) * [Apostles' Fast](/wiki/Apostles%27_Fast "Apostles' Fast") (*Eastern*) * [Dormition Fast](/wiki/Dormition_Fast "Dormition Fast") (*Eastern*) * [Elijah–Cross–Moses](/w/index.php?title=Elijah%E2%80%93Cross%E2%80%93Moses&action=edit&redlink=1 "Elijah–Cross–Moses (page does not exist)") (*East Syriac*) * [Dedication of the Church](/w/index.php?title=Season_of_the_Dedication_of_the_Church&action=edit&redlink=1 "Season of the Dedication of the Church (page does not exist)") (*Syriac*) |
| * [v](/wiki/Template:Liturgical_year "Template:Liturgical year") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Liturgical_year "Template talk:Liturgical year") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Liturgical_year "Special:EditPage/Template:Liturgical year") |

A **triduum** ([/ˈtrɪd.juəm, ˈtrɪdʒuəm/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); pl. **tridua**) is a religious observance that lasts three days.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Major tridua

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triduum&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Major tridua")]

[![refer to caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Holy_week_illustrations.png/250px-Holy_week_illustrations.png)](/wiki/File:Holy_week_illustrations.png)

Illustrations of various Catholic observances of the Paschal Triduum, including (2) the candles of Tenebrae services; (3) the reading of the Passion narrative; (6) the veneration of the cross; (7) the blessing of fire; (8) the blessing of baptismal water; (9) the Paschal candle; (10) the reading of the Four Lessons; (11) the commemoration of the Resurrection.

The best-known and most significant example today is the [liturgical](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") [Paschal Triduum](/wiki/Paschal_Triduum "Paschal Triduum") (the three days from the evening of [Maundy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday") to [Easter Sunday](/wiki/Easter "Easter")). Other liturgical tridua celebrated in [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") include the [Rogation Days](/wiki/Rogation_Days "Rogation Days") preceding [Ascension Thursday](/wiki/Ascension_Thursday "Ascension Thursday"), the feasts of [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") and [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") together with the first two days of their [octave](/wiki/Octave_(liturgy) "Octave (liturgy)"), and [Allhallowtide](/wiki/Allhallowtide "Allhallowtide") that lasts from [Halloween](/wiki/Halloween "Halloween") to [All Souls Day](/wiki/All_Souls_Day "All Souls Day").

In [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") (both [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") and [Catholic](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches")) the analogues of festive tridua take the form of a major feasts followed by an associated [Synaxis](/wiki/Synaxis "Synaxis"). The most publicly celebrated examples are the feast of [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)") together with its eve and the following day dedicated to Saint [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist"), and the [Nativity feast](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") with [Christmas Eve](/wiki/Christmas_Eve "Christmas Eve") and the Synaxis of [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos"). In Spain, tridua are celebrated in honor of each sacred image, for instance, a brotherhood has a fixed date of tridua in honor of their Christ or Virgin.

Ecclesiastical approval has been granted for tridua in honour of the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity"), the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") and [Saint Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph "Saint Joseph"). Tridua are also celebrated on the local level, often preceding the feasts of patron saints of parishes and localities. An example would be the [Obando Fertility Rites](/wiki/Obando_Fertility_Rites "Obando Fertility Rites") in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), which commemorate [Saint Paschal Baylon](/wiki/Saint_Paschal_Baylon "Saint Paschal Baylon"), [Saint Clare of Assisi](/wiki/Saint_Clare_of_Assisi "Saint Clare of Assisi") (in addition to her actual feast day), and [Our Lady of Salambáo](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Salambao "Our Lady of Salambao") for three consecutive days in mid-May.

## Other occasions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triduum&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Other occasions")]

Many other tridua are celebrated on occasions such as when children are preparing for their [First Communion](/wiki/First_Communion "First Communion"); among pupils as well as seminarians at the beginning of the [school year](/wiki/School_year "School year"); and in religious communities for those who are preparing to renew their vows annually or every six months.

## Historical tridua

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triduum&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Historical tridua")]

A triduum was held in [Limerick](/wiki/Limerick "Limerick") in 1867 in honor of [Our Lady of Perpetual Succour](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Perpetual_Succour "Our Lady of Perpetual Succour"), at which the associated icon was first displayed for public veneration in Ireland.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In January of 1915, a number of German cities observed a triduum of meditation on [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), an event which was influential on a young [Martin Heidegger](/wiki/Martin_Heidegger "Martin Heidegger").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[James G. McGarry](/wiki/James_G._McGarry "James G. McGarry"), a Catholic priest and professor, organized a 1977 triduum observance at [Knock Shrine](/wiki/Knock_Shrine "Knock Shrine").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triduum&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: See also")]
